title: LGBTQ activists invite drag queen group to all ages ‘Rainbow Harvest’ in Palmer
summary: A controversial LGBTQ activist group known as The Queen’s Guard staged a “Rainbow Harvest Festival” in downtown Palmer on Oct. 12. Boosted by recent funding from the Mat-Su Health Foundation, the group increasingly targets gender-confused youth and young adults through events, meetings and role-play...

title: Election Law, Roads, Wildfire, and lots more!
summary: Dear friends and neighbors, This week my dogs and I encountered this moose family (dad, mom, 2 kids) this week! What fun to see a whole family (from a distance, and we left the area promptly)! The post Election Law, Roads, Wildfire, and lots more! appeared first on Alaska Senate Majority .

title: How Nome is preparing for ex-typhoon Halong
summary: Days after a low-pressure system plowed through the Bering Strait, western Alaska communities are already preparing for another major storm. 50 mph wind gusts whipped communities across the Bering Strait region. Severe flooding left huge chunks of Kotzebue and Shishmaref underwater. Friday in Nome, it was both the calm after the storm and before the storm. Water levels had receded and evacuation not...

title: Wills & Veterans Land Applications
summary: Chugachmiut and Alaska Legal Services at AFNOctober 16-17, 2025Dena’ina Center, Anchorage Chugachmiut Realty will be at the Alaska Federation of Natives (AFN) Convention to assist Chugachmiut region members with Veterans […] The post Wills & Veterans Land Applications first appeared on Chugachmiut .

title: 2025 CBJ Municipal Election – Preliminary Unofficial Results as of 10/10/2025
summary: The City & Borough of Juneau (CBJ) Election Staff are continuing to process ballots from the October 7 2025, Regular Municipal Election. Updated preliminary results, including all ballots reviewed, approved, a nd scanned through Friday, October 10, are now available online: https://juneau.org/clerk/...

title: Accountant Reform Bill Becomes Law
summary: HOME | Page 19 Accountant Reform Bill Becomes Law Photo Credit: nateemee | Envato Add more accountants to the “credit” column of Alaska’s professional workforce. A new law reforms standards for certified public accountants (CPAs) to work in the state. House Bill 121 ( HB 121 ) passed the Alaska Legislature this spring without any votes against it, and it became law in July without Governor Mike Dunleavy’s signature. This week, supporters of HB 121 celebrated the reforms in advance of the law taking effect with the new year. What Count...

title: AST Fail to Register Sex Offender: Fairbanks [AK25103316]
summary: On 10/10/2025 Alaska State Troopers contacted Christopher Ozella on a traffic stop in Fairbanks. It was revealed that Christopher was out of compliance with the Sex Offender Registry and was remanded to FCC without incident.
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

entities.address_detail: a traffic stop in Fairbanks

title: October 2025 Newsletter
summary: New Council Members and Election of Officers | Council Response to EO 14276 | MRA Adjustments | Draft 2026 Annual Deployment Plan | BSAI Crab Specifications | Crab Crew Quota Shares | Groundfish Harvest Specification | Economic Data Reports Removal | BSAI Pacific Cod Pot Gear Limited Access Privilege Program (LAPP) | IFQ/CQE Transfer, Beneficiary Changes | Programmatic Evaluation | Staff Tasking | Upcoming Meetings New Council Members and Election of Officers At this meeting, the Council re-elected Angel Drobnica as Chair and elected Rachel Baker as Vice Chair. Ms. Drobnica, from Juneau, has long participated in the Council process as an Advisory Panel member, Chair of the AP, and Chair of the Council. She serves as the Director of Fisheries and Government Affairs for the Aleutian Pribilof Island Community Development Association. Ms. Baker is the Deputy Commissioner of the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, and represents the State of Alaska’s interests in federal fisheries managemen...

title: AST Warrant Arrest: Wasilla [AK25103233]
summary: On 10/10/25 at approximately 1455 hours Palmer AST responded to an address in Williwaw for a suspicious circumstance. During the investigation it was learned that two individuals, both with outstanding warrants for their arrests had recently been in the area. With assistance from Wasilla Police Department a vehicle of interest was located leaving the area and ultimately contacted at the Fishook Food Mart near Wasilla Fishook and Seldon. AST and WPD ultimately located Kenneth Jenks, age 37 of Wasilla, hiding in the woods behind the store. Jenks had two warrants for his arrest, the first for original charges of 4 counts of Misconduct Involving Controlled Substance 5th Degree with a bail of $250, the second for original charge of Misconduct Involving Weapons 3rd Degree with a bail of $500. Je
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

charges: 4 counts of Misconduct Involving Controlled Substance 5th Degree with a bail of $250
the second for original charge of Misconduct Involving Weapons 3rd Degree with a bail of $500

title: AST Warrant Arrest: Wasilla [AK25086288]
summary: On 10/10/25 at approximately 1319 hours Palmer AST performed a routine traffic stop on a vehicle on Copper Creek Rd and N Williwaw Way. Investigation revealed a passenger in the vehicle, Seth Rogers, age 35 of Wasilla had an outstanding warrant for his arrest for failing to appear on original charges of Misconduct Involving Controlled Substances 5th Degree. Rogers was arrested and transported to Mat Su Pretrial where he was remanded and held on $250 bail.
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

title: AST Huslia Assault of a Child: Huslia, AK [AK25102978]
summary: Galena Troopers received a report from the Huslia TPO that Athena Sam had assaulted her 11 year old son at their residence in Huslia. title: Amid government shutdown, Alaska Airlines pledges to continue Cordova service
summary: Alaska Airlines has pledged to continue its essential air service to and from Cordova amid this latest government shutdown. The air carrier – the only one to provide regular service in and out of town – said the federal Department of Transportation would relieve Alaska Airlines of its essential air...

title: Trump again green lights Ambler Road
summary: President Donald Trump has again approved plans for the proposed 211-mile Ambler Road through wilderness lands – including those owned by two Alaska Native regional firms – to reach a lucrative mining site. The road and the project itself “is something that should’ve been long operating and making billions of dollars for our country and supplying a lot of energy and minerals,” Trump said on Monday in an Oval Office ceremony, giving his stamp of approval again for the project he approved in his first term. When former President Joe Biden took over, however, he gave it a thumbs down. The road would run from the Dalton Highway nearly to Kotzebue, with construction costs...

title: ‘Substantial’ layoffs of federal workers launched by Trump administration amid shutdown
summary: Protesters rally outside of the Theodore Roosevelt Federal Building headquarters of the U.S. Office of Personnel Management on Feb. 5, 2025 in Washington, DC. (Photo by Alex Wong/Getty Images) This report has been updated. WASHINGTON — The Trump administration announced Friday it had begun mass layoffs of federal employees, a step not taken during previous government shutdowns and one that could significantly reshape the size and scope of government. White House budget director Russ Vought posted on social media mid-day that Reductions in Force, the technical name for layoffs, had started. “The RIFs have begun,” Vought wrote. Vought didn’t share any other details on social media and a budget office spokesperson only said that the layoffs would be “substantial” after States Newsroom asked for information about how many federal workers and which departments would be impacted. The Trump administration outlined its current layoff plans later in the day in a filing required in a federal cou...

title: Shutdown forces Medicare patients off popular telehealth and hospital-at-home programs
summary: Robert Thornton received personalized hospital care for COVID-19 and pneumonia in his Belvidere, Ill., home in 2024 as part of a Medicare in-home care program that expired October 1. (Photo courtesy of OSF Healthcare) The federal government shutdown is forcing a reckoning for two remote health care programs because they automatically expired Oct. 1. The telehealth and in-home hospital care programs were both temporary — but increasingly popular — options for Medicare recipients. They allowed doctors and hospitals to bill Medicare for telehealth appointments and in-home visits from nurses to provide care that is generally only available in hospitals. The shutdown has prevented Congress from extending them. More than 4 million Medicare beneficiaries used telehealth services in the...
